title: Expressing support for the strengthening of United States-Africa partnerships in critical minerals development.
plain_summary: This resolution asks Congress to support working more closely with African countries to get important minerals that America needs. These minerals are used for things like phones, computers, and military equipment that keep our country safe and help our economy. The government wants to make a five-year plan to help American companies invest in mining projects in Africa. This would help America get these minerals from friendly countries instead of depending too much on China for them.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for stronger partnerships between the United States and African countries to develop critical minerals.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Critical minerals are materials that are essential for America's national security and economy. Many of the supply chains that American companies and the military depend on are controlled by foreign countries that may not have America's best interests in mind, especially companies from China. The Chinese government controls or influences many businesses that could harm American interests. Africa has about thirty percent of the world's proven critical mineral reserves, and at least twenty African countries export minerals that America considers critical for security and economic reasons. These minerals like cobalt, lithium, and nickel are needed for clean energy technology, electronics, and defense equipment. Some African countries want to increase investment in mining and processing these minerals to create more jobs and economic growth in their own countries. When mining is done responsibly with good environmental practices and fair labor standards, it can create government income, jobs, and help communities develop. However, poorly managed mining often leads to corruption, worker abuse, environmental damage, and violent conflicts. In twenty twenty-two, the United States started the Mineral Security Partnership with thirteen other countries and the European Union to encourage responsible mining and investment in critical mineral supply chains. That same year, America signed an agreement with the Democratic Republic of Congo and Zambia to help them build supply chains for electric vehicle batteries. International experts predict that between one hundred eighty billion and two hundred twenty billion dollars will be invested in critical mineral mining from twenty twenty-two to twenty thirty, but only ten percent of that money is planned for Africa. In twenty twenty-three, America had to import eighty-two percent or more of at least twenty-nine critical minerals from other countries, including fourteen rare earth elements. China's control over critical mineral supply chains poses serious national security risks to the United States.

sections.title: House Position
sections.text: The House of Representatives believes that American policy should work to diversify where we get our critical minerals so that foreign countries of concern cannot restrict our access to these important materials or make them more expensive. The policy should improve how well federal agencies work together to help American businesses invest in critical minerals in countries that are friendly to the United States, including through the Mineral Security Partnership. America should also strengthen partnerships with African countries that produce or have reserves of critical minerals by supporting investments in new or expanded mining and processing projects in African markets. This would help create fair and transparent access to African mineral production and promote responsible mining and processing that adds value in Africa. The government should provide incentives like financing or technical help for American businesses and businesses in friendly African countries to invest in mineral projects that are strategically important to the United States. The administration should turn the agreement with the Democratic Republic of Congo and Zambia about electric vehicle battery supply chains into a real investment program that can be used as a model across the continent.

sections.title: Five-Year Strategy Request
sections.text: The House urges the Secretary of State to work with the Secretaries of Commerce, Energy, Interior, Treasury, and Defense, plus other relevant agencies, to create a five-year strategy. This strategy should strengthen American commercial diplomacy and development efforts to support American critical mineral investors and help solve the challenges of doing business in African countries. It should provide financing and technical assistance to help eligible African mineral producers expand their mining and processing abilities, which would diversify American critical mineral supply chains and help African countries add more value to their resources. The strategy should support diversified investment funds through the United States International Development Finance Corporation and other federal agencies that provide money for or encourage American private companies to invest in future mining projects in African countries. Finally, it should improve economic cooperation with eligible African critical mineral producers.
